coredata.dc:title: In-vitro degradation and corrosion protection of chitosan composite coatings containing hydroxyapatite or bioactive glass on Ti6Al4V implants: A comparative study

coredata.dc:description: This research systematically evaluates the biodegradation and corrosion behavior of a novel class of composite coatings for bone implants based on chitosan (CS) and two bioactive components (Hydroxyapatite (HA) and Bioactive glass (BG)) in simulated physiological conditions through a comparative approach. Chitosan (CS)-based composite coatings (HACS and BGCS) were deposited on Ti6Al4V substrates via electrophoretic deposition (EPD) using unified processing parameters. Initially, the primary materials, relevant suspensions, and final coatings were studied and compared by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), Zetasizer, and X-ray diffractometer (XRD) methods. While positive ZPs for both HACS (55.44 ± 2.43 mV) and BGCS (35.62 ± 2.95 mV) suspensions were aligned with cathodic deposition of coatings, a large difference between recorded current densities during EPD for HACS (6.98 ± 0.38 mA·cm−2) and BGCS (56.14 ± 3.99 mA·cm−2) were noteworthy. The in-vitro degradation of BGCS and HACS coatings in DMEM solution was assessed not only under a conventional static condition but also within a fluid-dynamic degradation setup. The mass and pH evolution, surface morphology, compositional changes, and release of metallic ions were investigated over the degradation period by gravimetric, optical microscope, SEM, FTIR, and ICP-MS techniques, respectively. While the majority of BGCS degradation (above 3 mg·cm−2) occurred only after 8 h of immersion, a maximum mass loss of about 2 mg·cm−2 happened for HACS coatings on day 10 of the test. Electrochemical corrosion studies of coated samples compared to plain substrates in DMEM solution were carried out using open circuit potential (OCP), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests, and the equivalent circuit was defined to model the experimental data. Interestingly, the corrosion rate of the uncoated sample (9.13E−05 mm·year−1) was about three times and ten times less than HACS and BGCS samples, respectively. It was revealed that the surface pretreatment (polishing) method can have a decisive effect on the corrosion protection behavior of Ti6Al4V samples, coated with CS-based EPD coatings.

head.ce:title: In-vitro degradation and corrosion protection of chitosan composite coatings containing hydroxyapatite or bioactive glass on Ti6Al4V implants: A comparative study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research systematically evaluates the biodegradation and corrosion behavior of a novel class of composite coatings for bone implants based on chitosan (CS) and two bioactive components (Hydroxyapatite (HA) and Bioactive glass (BG)) in simulated physiological conditions through a comparative approach. Chitosan (CS)-based composite coatings (HACS and BGCS) were deposited on Ti6Al4V substrates via electrophoretic deposition (EPD) using unified processing parameters. Initially, the primary materials, relevant suspensions, and final coatings were studied and compared by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), Zetasizer, and X-ray diffractometer (XRD) methods. While positive ZPs for both HACS (55.44 ± 2.43 mV) and BGCS (35.62 ± 2.95 mV) suspensions were aligned with cathodic deposition of coatings, a large difference between recorded current densities during EPD for HACS (6.98 ± 0.38 mA·cm−2) and BGCS (56.14 ± 3.99 mA·cm−2) were noteworthy. The in-vitro degradation of BGCS and HACS coatings in DMEM solution was assessed not only under a conventional static condition but also within a fluid-dynamic degradation setup. The mass and pH evolution, surface morphology, compositional changes, and release of metallic ions were investigated over the degradation period by gravimetric, optical microscope, SEM, FTIR, and ICP-MS techniques, respectively. While the majority of BGCS degradation (above 3 mg·cm−2) occurred only after 8 h of immersion, a maximum mass loss of about 2 mg·cm−2 happened for HACS coatings on day 10 of the test. Electrochemical corrosion studies of coated samples compared to plain substrates in DMEM solution were carried out using open circuit potential (OCP), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests, and the equivalent circuit was defined to model the experimental data. Interestingly, the corrosion rate of the uncoated sample (9.13E−05 mm·year−1) was about three times and ten times less than HACS and BGCS samples, respectively. It was revealed that the surface pretreatment (polishing) method can have a decisive effect on the corrosion protection behavior of Ti6Al4V samples, coated with CS-based EPD coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biodegradation is one of the key properties of coatings developed for bone-substituting metallic implants, as it directly influences critical functions such as integration with surrounding tissues, release of therapeutic agents, and regulation of the corrosion behavior of metallic substrates. The release of biologically active ions from bioactive ceramic coatings stimulates the bone healing process around the implant until it is covered with the newly formed tissue. A comparable degradation rate of the coating with that of the tissue regeneration leads to a robust bond between bone and implant that determines the ultimate implant success [1–3]. Biomedical coatings are considered a promising platform for drug delivery applications. In this regard, the degradation kinetics of the coating should be tailored to match the required dosage over time to fulfill its therapeutic task followed by safe elimination from the body [4]. The biodegradation rate also affects corrosion phenomena that occur on the metal surface when it is in contact with body fluids, where aggressive species such as chloride ions are present. A proper balance between the rate of coatings' degradation and the regeneration of new tissues around the metallic implant can control the release of potentially harmful ions from metals and alloys into body fluids [5,6].

To achieve this balance, bioactive ceramics and bioactive glasses play a crucial role, as they not only provide structural support but also initiate essential chemical and biological responses that strengthen the bond between the implant and the surrounding tissue [5]. Calcium phosphate ceramics such as hydroxyapatite (HA) and bioactive glasses (BGs) are well-known bioactive materials that have been employed in various forms for bone regeneration applications including as coatings on metallic substrates [7,8]. HA (Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2), which shares chemical similarity with the mineral phase of bone, improves the osseointegration process by reacting with physiological fluids to facilitate bonding with both hard and soft tissues. On the other hand, BGs offer exceptional properties such as superior bioactivity and gene activation. The bone regenerative capability of BGs stems from the precipitation of an amorphous calcium phosphate layer that later transforms into nanocrystalline hydroxy-carbonate apatite, which enables chemical bonding to native tissues [1]. In addition, BGs can release biologically active ions to induce osteogenesis and angiogenesis processes, which are essential for effective bone regeneration.

Building on this concept, composite coatings based on natural polymers, bioactive ceramics, and glasses have emerged as a new class of surface modifications for bone-substitute metallic implants, offering enhanced integration with host tissue [9]. The combination of bioactive inorganic materials with biodegradable polymers results in synergistic benefits from both components, ensuring improved biocompatibility and controlled degradation rates. While polymers can be exploited to tune the degradation rate of bioactive coatings and enhance the corrosion protection of the substrate, the inorganic bioactive particles can increase the overall mechanical strength of the coating and offset the pH drop induced by acidic degradation products of the polymer components (in case of synthetic polymers) [10,11]. Moreover, incorporating biopolymers into the coating composition is an effective strategy to augment the consolidation of ceramic particles and their adhesion to the substrate, as well as the acceleration of the protein adsorption due to their functional groups (e.g., carboxyl or hydroxyl groups) [12,13]. Chitosan (CS), a natural polysaccharide produced from the deacetylation of sustainable chitin sources (e.g. the exoskeleton of crustaceans, the fungal cell wall), has been extensively adopted for developing composite coatings [4,9]. A unique combination of features including tunable biodegradation rate, good biocompatibility, antimicrobial, and mucoadhesive properties has broadened the application of CS over the last decades expanding from bone and wound healing to drug delivery and tissue engineering fields [1,5].

To harness these advantages in implant coatings, electrophoretic deposition (EPD) represents one of the most effective techniques for depositing organic-inorganic composite coatings, ensuring precise control over composition and structure. The advantages that make EPD a favorable coating method include high processing efficiency, short deposition time, operational simplicity, room-temperature processing, low cost, and high uniformity and controllability of coating thickness (0.3–100 μm) [14,15]. Although there are many research papers about the development and characterization of composite coatings based on chitosan and inorganic bioactive HA and/or BG particles [5,11,13,16], none of which has systematically evaluated biodegradation and corrosion behavior of these fundamental coatings in simulated physiological conditions through a comparative approach. Understanding these properties is essential for assessing the long-term stability and functionality of biomaterials in clinical applications.

In-vitro degradation studies of biomaterials are of great importance, as they affects cellular responses, biocompatibility, drug release profile, and the functions of a biomaterial as a whole [1]. One of the important criteria in in-vitro studies is the selection of a suitable test solution. A variety of test fluids have been introduced [17], which have been considered for degradation studies; such as phosphate buffering solution (PBS) [18], simulated body fluid (SBF) [19], synthetic saliva [20], Hank's balanced salt solution (HBSS) [21], and Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) [22]. The fluid within our body is a complex system with strong buffering ability, with the pH ranging between 7.35 and 7.45. The buffering agents in simulated biological fluids influence the degradation behavior of biomaterials, hence, the selection of a fluid with the highest possible similarity to body fluids is important to carry out more accurate analysis [17,23]. Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane is often used in the preparation of SBF while it is not present in human plasma. On the other hand, buffering agents including HEPES, hydrocarbonates and a trace amount of hydrophosphates, exist in DMEM [17]. Furthermore, among available physiological-like media, DMEM contains glucose together with various amino acids. These organic components have been reported to influence the degradation of biomedical alloys like magnesium [24]. Despite its highest similarity to the composition of human plasma among available fluids, the number of research studies considered DMEM as a test solution for degradation and corrosion analysis of biomaterials is limited. One of the expected advantages of coatings on metallic implants is preventing or restricting the release of metallic ions from the substrate, particularly those with possible toxic effects in the human body. Biodegradation studies help in predicting how useful the coatings are in hindering the release of harmful ions. Consequently, the choice of test solution has an impact on the results of corrosion studies as well. In a study conducted by Xin et al. [17] the corrosion rate of AZ91 Mg alloy in five different physiological-like solutions was studied. They observed the most negative corrosion potential in samples soaked in DMEM and recommended it as the most suitable medium for in-vitro degradation testing, as it gives more realistic results for biocorrosion studies. Building on these findings, further investigation is required to systematically assess how composite coatings perform in such conditions, particularly with respect to their biodegradation and corrosion resistance.

This research aims to systematically investigate the biodegradation behavior under both static and dynamic conditions, as well as the corrosion protection capabilities of advanced composite coatings composed of chitosan and either bioactive glass or hydroxyapatite. These coatings are deposited on Ti6Al4V alloy using the EPD method, with DMEM as the testing medium to ensure a realistic simulation of physiological conditions. A key goal of the study is to compare, within a consistent experimental setting, the performance of CS-based composite coatings incorporating HA or BG particles as inorganic additive.

Commercial melt-derived 45S5 bioactive glass particles (SCHOTT Vitryxx®, Germany) with a composition of 45 SiO2, 24.5 CaO, 24.5 Na2O, 6 P2O5 (wt%), and hydroxyapatite particles (CAS-No. 1306-06-5, Sigma-Aldrich) with chemical formula Ca5(PO4)3OH, both with particle sizes in the range of 4 ≤ d50 ≤ 6 μm were used as bioactive inorganic components of composite coatings. Medium molecular weight chitosan powder (DD = 75–85 %, Sigma-Aldrich) was employed as the organic matrix of the coatings. The substrates were discs of Ti6Al4V alloy with a diameter of 10 mm and a thickness of 1.5 mm. The heavy oxide layer on Ti6Al4V discs was removed by SiC paper (P320-P3000 grit sizes) towards a mirror-polished surface (Fig. 1 ), to minimize the insulating effect of this layer during electrophoretic deposition [25]. Afterward, the substrates were ultrasonically rinsed in ethanol, acetone, and water for 5 min each, respectively. Finally, the substrates were dried with compressed N2 gas and kept for further processing with EPD.

To prepare colloidal suspensions for EPD, 0.5 g/L CS was dissolved in diluted acetic acid (1 %), then the solution was adjusted to its final volume by adding 79 % v/v methanol (≥99.9 %). After that, 2 g/L of relevant bioactive particles were added to obtain individual BGCS and HACS suspensions. Prepared solutions underwent sequential stirring and sonication steps, so stabilized colloidal suspensions could be obtained [26]. In the BGCS suspension, 0.4 % v/v H2O2 was further added to control the rate of gas bubble formation during EPD and improve the mechanical strength of corresponding coatings. The use of hydrogen peroxide has been shown to reduce H2 bubble formation during EPD by producing many hydroxyl radicals [27]. The Ti6Al4V substrates were coated using relevant suspensions in a standard two-electrode EPD system. The counter electrode was 316 L stainless steel foil placed at a 1 cm distance from the working electrode. The DC-potential and duration of the cathodic EPD were 25 V and 120 s, respectively. For the aim of comparison, unified deposition parameters were selected following trials and errors to achieve a trade-off between desired properties in the two coatings. After the coating process, the samples were placed horizontally overnight at room temperature to dry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Primary materials, EPD suspensions and coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Before conducting systematic biodegradation tests on BGCS and HACS coatings as well as the electrochemical corrosion studies in a physiological-like condition, the primary materials, the relevant suspensions, and the developed coatings were thoroughly characterized using various techniques. The morphology of the starting bioactive particles together with microstructures and thickness of the composite coatings were analyzed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (AURIGA 4750, ZEISS), equipped with an energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) detector for a compositional study. To view cross-sectional SEM images, special sample holders were used to fix coated samples on the side view using a screw. The electrophysical characteristics of both types of suspensions prepared for EPD were assessed in terms of zeta potential, electrophoretic mobility, and conductivity by Zetasizer Nano (ZSP, Malvern Instruments) (n = 5). Moreover, the electrical current densities during the coating process were monitored and recorded using a bench-top digital multimeter (TTi 1906, Thurlby Thandar Instruments). The components of the coating systems prior and following EPD were further characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (MiniFlex 600, Rigaku) with Cu Kα radiation in the range of 2θ = 10°–90°, speed of 3 deg·min−1, and step size of 0.02°. The surface wettability of coatings compared to the uncoated Ti6Al4V substrate was evaluated by contact angle measurements using a goniometer and the sessile drop technique (DSA30E, KRÜSS). Measurements were carried out using a deionized water droplet (Vol. 10 μL) and on three samples from each group. The adhesion strength of coatings to the substrate was assessed by the tape test (Cross-hatch cutter kit, Elcometer 107) based on the ISO 2409 standard procedure [28]. The coatings were inspected using light microscopy (M50, Leica Microsystems) following the test, and adhesion strength was rated between “0” and “5” according to the standard description.

Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM/F12 (1:1), Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific) was selected as the medium for degradation studies of the composite coatings, owing to its close compositional similarity to the body plasma [17]. The other parameters were also designed to mimic the body environment to the best possible extent in both static and dynamic conditions. A comparison between compositions of DMEM medium and blood plasma is provided in Table 1 . The uncoated sides of samples used in these tests were sealed using water-resistant lacquer spray (341036, Primastar).

To study the in-vitro degradation of composite coatings based on either BG or HA inorganic particles, every coated sample (n = 2) was immersed in 10 mL of DMEM solution at 37.5 °C up to 10 days. The samples were carefully taken out at every predetermined time point, rinsed with deionized water, and dried in a desiccator. Several characterization techniques were employed to analyze and compare the biodegradation process of HACS and BGCS coatings on Ti6Al4V substrates. The pH evolution of the test solution and mass variations of coated samples at every interval were recorded by a pH meter (S220, Mettler-Toledo) and a precision balance (EP214, Explorer Pro, OHAUS) with a resolution of 0.5 mg. The flow of coating degradation was inspected visually, and the applicable optical microscopy images (Stemi 2000-C, ZEISS) were annexed. The surface morphology evolution of the coatings over immersion time was studied by FE-SEM images. Additionally, to inspect the degradation/precipitation process further, the leftovers on the substrates after each time point were scratched off, and their chemical composition was analyzed using attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) (IRAffinity-1S, SHIMADZU) in the range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 wavenumber. Finally, the cumulative metallic ions released from the coated and uncoated substrates were evaluated by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (8800 Triple Quadrupole, Agilent Technologies) from diluted DMEM solutions that were in contact with respective samples.

A custom-made setup developed to study fluid-dynamic degradation of various biomaterials [31], was used to investigate the stability of composite coatings on Ti6Al4V coupons in contact with circulating DMEM. A total volume of 500 mL DMEM was used for five samples fixed in a cuboid chamber with the coated side orthogonal to the flow direction. The flow rate of 150 mL·min−1 was maintained using a peristaltic pump (IP 65, MCP-Process, ISMATEC), and the pH of the working fluid was buffered at 7.4 by adding 0.1 M NaOH and HCl solutions as needed. The pH, pressure, flow rate, and fluid temperature were monitored through the application of dedicated sensors. A schematic illustration of the fluid-dynamic experimental setup is demonstrated in Fig. 2 . At two time points (24 and 96 h), the samples were carefully removed from the sample holder, rinsed with deionized water, and placed in a desiccator to dry. At each time point, the mass evolution of samples was gravimetrically measured. The microstructure and morphology of coatings were analyzed by images from optical and scanning electron microscopes. Moreover, the concentration of metallic ions released in the fluid from Ti6Al4V substrates covered with either HACS or BGCS coatings was studied by the ICP-MS technique.

Biocorrosion is one of the key forms of degradation in metallic implants, which determines their functionality, durability, and biocompatibility in vivo [32]. Surface treatments and coatings of metallic implants affect their corrosion behavior and, consequently, biodegradation of coating materials will indirectly influence corrosion performance. Electrochemical corrosion studies of coated samples (HACS and BGCS) compared to the plain substrate (mirror-polished Ti6Al4V) were performed in DMEM. A three-electrode electrochemical workstation was used with the sample as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, and a Pt rod as the counter electrode. The corrosion measurements including open circuit potential (OCP), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted using a potentiostat device (Autolab PGSTAT204, Metrohm), and corresponding curves and the equivalent circuit were analyzed by the Nova 2.1.7 software. The samples were in contact with DMEM for 1 h before electrochemical measurements, and the OCP was recorded for 1400 s following the immersion time. The PDP was performed in the range from −0.5 to 2 V versus OCP at a scan rate of 1 mV·s−1. The EIS test was done in a frequency range of 100 kHz–10 mHz, measured around OCP and under AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Starting materials, EPD suspensions and coatings
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the commercially obtained bioactive particles was analyzed via scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 3 ). Complying with previous reports [33,34], BG particles have a polygonal morphology, as expected due to the fabrication process (melting). At a higher magnification (Fig. 3 (B)), the individual BG particles appear to be compact with a flake-like surface structure. On the other hand, the HA particles appear as spheroids with a fairly large size distribution. In a closer view (Fig. 3 (D)), the needle-like nanocrystals of HA are discernible, giving a fine porous appearance to the spheroidal particles.

To study the electrophoresis process of charged HA and BG particles, the electrophysical properties (Zeta potential, mobility, and conductivity) of the relevant colloidal suspensions were measured (Table 2 ). The average variation in current densities measured during the deposition process from the HACS and BGCS suspensions has also been provided. The zeta potentials (ZPs) obtained for both suspensions are positive confirming the coverage of particles with protonated chitosan chains that lead to a cathodic electrophoretic deposition [9]. The absolute value of ZP in HACS, however, is greater than that in the BGCS suspension, which affirms that both higher particle surface charge and colloidal stability pertain to the HACS suspension [35]. The electrophoretic mobility (Mob.) of the two types of bioactive particles in CS solution shows a similar trend as it directly correlates with the ZP through the equation below [36]:
(1)

where ϵ 0 is permittivity of vacuum, ϵ
r is relative permittivity of the solvent, η is the solvent's viscosity, and f(κr) = 1.5 or 1, for a point or flat surfaces, respectively.

In contrast, the electrical conductivity, which is correlated with the capability of a suspension for electrical current transmission [26], has a higher value in the BGCS suspension compared to the HA counterpart. The conductivity increases with rising the concentration of ions [37]. Correspondingly, the higher conductivity of BGCS suspension can be attributed to a faster dissolution of BG particles into abundant ions on the grounds of its amorphous nature. It is worth noting that the desired properties for an EPD suspension are reported to be low viscosity, high dielectric constant, and low conductivity [37]. The current density during EPD of BGCS was remarkably higher than that of HACS suspension (Table 2). The electric current in an EPD electrolyte is carried not only by charged particles but also by free ions coexisting in the suspension [37]. This outcome further confirms that faster dissolution of BG particles contributes to a significant increase in current density with possible adverse effects on the microstructure of the resultant coating, as water electrolysis and thus gas evolution proceed faster on the cathode surface [38].

The SEM images of electrophoretically deposited composite coatings based on CS polymer and bioactive particles are presented in Fig. 4 (A, B). In both coatings, a uniform distribution of inorganic bioactive particles can be noticed yet with different structures. It has been proposed that in an EPD process, the particles can have a stable, flocculated, or coagulated distribution structure on the surface, depending on different factors including pH, electrolyte concentration, current density, and mass flow [37]. A highly ordered deposit with a maximum packing density is a characteristic of stable distribution, which is consistent with the morphology of the HACS coatings (Fig. 4 (B)). On the other hand, the microstructure of BGCS coatings can be seen in a type of coagulated state, where non-uniform packings of large colloid clusters are distributed over the coating (Fig. 4 (A)). The distribution state of coatings has also influenced their thickness. The cross-sectional images of HACS and BGCS coatings in Fig. S1 show a fluffy structure in the BGCS coating, while the HACS has a compact and dense structure. Likewise, the thickness of BGCS (64.82 ± 4.33 μm) is slightly higher than that of HACS (42.71 ± 1.81 μm). Considering the incorporation of micron-sized particles in the coatings, the measured thicknesses are comparable to those reported in similar coating systems [39,40].

The EDS analysis of BGCS and HACS composite coatings is presented in Fig. 4 (C) and (D), respectively. In addition to the common peaks corresponding to the titanium alloy (Ti6Al4V) and the CS matrix, characteristic peaks of Si, Na, P, and Ca distinguish the BGCS from the HACS coatings, which exhibit sharp P and Ca peaks. Furthermore, despite taking advantage of hydrogen peroxide to restrict the rate of bubble formation, microstructural irregularities (e.g. cracks, bubble footprints, and blisters) could be detected in some areas within the BGCS coating (Fig. 4 (E)). The structure of HACS at the same magnification is also presented (Fig. 4 (F)) with the aim of comparison.

XRD patterns of HACS and BGCS coatings together with the uncoated Ti6Al4V substrate are displayed in Fig. 5 (A). The diffraction patterns of primary powders (BG, CS, HA) used in the preparation of the coatings are also presented (Fig. 5 (B, C, D)). Ti6Al4V is a titanium alloy consisting of both hexagonal α and cubic β crystalline phases. JCPDS cards # 44-1294 and # 44-1288 correspond to Ti-α and Ti-β, respectively, from which the characteristic peaks in the XRD pattern of this alloy can be detected [41]. The peaks associated with crystalline HA were identified according to the JCPDS standard (# 09-0432) (Fig. 5 (D)) [42–44]. The broad peak in the range of 2θ = 20°–34° represents the amorphous silicate structure of BG (Fig. 5 (B)) [45,46]. The characteristic peaks of semi-crystalline CS are located at 2θ = 12° and 2θ = 20° (Fig. 5 (C)) [47–49]. The XRD pattern is dominated by the characteristic peaks of the Ti6Al4V substrate (Fig. 5 (A)). On the other hand, the characteristic peaks related to crystalline HA are discernible in the XRD spectrum of HACS, yet with weaker intensity due to the low thickness (∼43 μm) of the composite coatings.

Surface wettability is one of the important properties that not only influences the biological responses to biomaterials [50,51], but also affects the degradation behavior of biodegradable coatings. As shown in Fig. S2, Contact angles of both HACS and BGCS coatings are very close (30.85 ± 2.41° and 29.52 ± 1.04°, respectively), in line with reported values for similar coating systems [51–53]. Surfaces with contact angles lower than 45° are defined as hydrophilic [26] and favorable for bone tissue regeneration [50]. Compared to the uncoated surface (62.96 ± 1.64°), coatings impart hydrophilicity to the substrate due to the hydrophilic nature of both BG [52] and HA [54] compositions.

The adhesion strength of HACS and BGCS composite coatings analyzed by the tape test showed that the adhesion strength of coatings was influenced by their microstructures. In HACS, flaking of the coating occurred only along the edges of the cuts (Class. 2 based on ISO 2409), while the affected area in BGCS was higher than 65 % (Class. 5 based on ISO 2409), in which the remaining coatings are specified with arrows (Fig. S3 (B)). Besides the requirement for improving the microstructure of coatings to improve their adhesion strength, another factor is the surface pretreatment of the substrate. It is proven that surface roughness can enhance real surface area and mechanical interlocking, resulting in higher adhesion strength [55,56]. Therefore, surface pretreatments that impart micro/nano roughness to the substrate, such as chemical etching and sand-blasting, might favor the adhesion strength of the coatings to metallic substrates.

The mass variations of BGCS and HACS composite coated samples over ten days of static immersion in DMEM were studied (Fig. 6 ). The majority of BGCS degradation (above 3 mg/cm2) occurred only after 8 h of immersion. Afterward, the BGCS coatings degraded slowly, and finally, they showed gaining traces of mass due to precipitations on the 10th day of the experiment. On the other hand, the degradation rate of HACS coatings was slower and a maximum mass loss of about 2 mg/cm2 was observed at the final time point of the test. One prominent reason for the contrary trend observed in the biodegradation of BGCS and HACS composite coatings is attributed to the difference in the reactivity (and bioreactivity) of HA and BG and thus their resorption rate, which influences the overall biodegradation kinetics of the corresponding coatings [57,58]. The changes in the pH of the medium in contact with BGCS and HACS samples were documented at each time point (Fig. 6 (B)). The pH evolution is correlated with the coatings' mass loss, eventually reaching a common end point at an acidic value. The changes in pH can be influenced by the degradation of the organic and the dissolution of the inorganic phases as well as the precipitation of compounds from DMEM onto the samples. The decreasing trend of the pH curves can be attributed to the dominance of acidic byproducts over the alkaline ions released from the inorganic compounds [59]. The degradation mechanism of CS in an in-vivo condition is primarily through enzymatic activities, particularly by lysozymes [60,61]. Generally, CS polymer degradation can occur through depolymerization, hydrolysis (enzymatic or non-enzymatic), and oxidation reactions. Degradation of CS results in the breakdown of the polymer into chitosan oligosaccharides of variable length. Further degradation of glucosamine and N-acetyl-glucosamine leads to the formation of free radicals and acidic compounds, which further increase the rate of degradation [60,62,63]. The acetic acid residues in the coatings from the deposition step could also affect the solution pH recorded over the test period. Accordingly, the required time to balance the pH values is expected to increase and the final equilibrium pH level tends to be lower. Motealleh et al. [19] studied degradation behavior of BG 45S5 scaffolds coated with different natural and synthetic polymers in SBF solution. The scaffolds coated with natural polymers not only showed faster weight loss than those coated with synthetic ones but also, in some cases, they degraded faster than the uncoated scaffolds. Therefore, while natural polymers in composite structures can control the dissolution rate of BG, a faster overall weight loss and pH drop are inevitable consequences of their use. The optical microscopy images taken from the samples at different intervals are presented in Fig. 6 (C). The changes in the appearance of samples well reflect the mass variation results. While degradation of BGCS has notably progressed after 8 h of exposure, the HACS coating nearly preserved the initial structure after 6 days of immersion in DMEM.

SEM images taken from the coatings at different times reflect the progress of the degradation of BGCS and HACS coatings, which is consistent with optical microscope images. The SEM micrographs of the coatings before the test (Fig. 7 (A, G)) reveal a balanced distribution of the two phases (i.e., CS and bioactive particles) in the composite. The swelling of the polymer due to liquid diffusion through the gaps and surface irregularities in BGCS coating was observed after one day of immersion (Fig. 7 (C)), which progressed rapidly and led to the removal of the coating (and exposure of the metallic substrate) in the following days (Fig. 7 (D, E)). Partial disruption of the coating layer facilitates further fluid penetration into the interface leading to an accelerated degradation rate [19]. At higher magnifications (Fig. 7 (D)), the morphology of CS matrix in the BGCS coating illustrates the progress of hydrolytic degradation in the shape of pore formation. On the other hand, the integrity of HACS coating was preserved for a longer period, which can be due to the more robust structure of the coating and reliable interlocking between HA particles and the CS matrix. However, slow reduction in the quantity of organic and inorganic compounds was perceived over the course of time. The surface morphology of the remaining parts of HACS and BGCS coatings after 10 days of immersion revealed a porous microstructure, which is formed due to the release of bioactive particles and degradation of chitosan. The former was predominant in HACS, while the latter was frequently noticed in BGCS considering the stages of degradation they were in. DMEM is a physiological-like solution enriched with different ions and organic species. The signs of precipitation from the solution can be identified at the final time points. Indeed, the formation of calcium phosphate precipitates by immersing the coatings composed of BG and HA bioactive particles in DMEM have been confirmed in previous studies [3,64].

At various time points, the chemical changes of the coatings were analyzed by scratching off the residual deposits on the surface, properly mixing the obtained powder and taking FTIR spectra. The results were compared with the FTIR spectra of the initial coatings before immersion in DMEM solution (Fig. 8 ). Considering the FTIR spectra of the primary BGCS coating, characteristic peaks of silica-based BG were clearly detected at 457 cm−1 and in the 1000–1100 cm−1 range, corresponding to νsym and νasym vibrations of SiOSi bonds, respectively [26,65]. Besides, several reflections with different intensities can be specified within the region of approximately 1340–1650 cm−1, originating from CO, NH, CN, and OH groups in the CS structure [26,66]. Similarly, a shallow band around 3000 cm−1 pertains to the stretching vibrations of CH bonds of CS [66,67]. Moreover, the individual peak at 873 cm−1 probably appeared due to the presence of non-bridging SiO groups in BG and/or COC groups of the polysaccharide structure [68,69].

Over the course of immersion, representative peaks of BG gradually fade away, indicating a faster dissolution rate of BG. On the other hand, new bands emerged and intensified as the immersion times prolonged, which are mostly due to precipitations from DMEM solutions on the coating. A double peak at approximately 1640 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 corresponds to amide groups. DMEM is a solution enriched with amino acids and vitamins; thus, the mentioned peaks can stem from stretching vibrations of CO groups in Asparagine, Glutamine, and Niacinamide formulations [70,71]. Similarly, a broad peak formed around 2900 cm−1 is due to vibrations of CH bands found in biochemical compounds coexisting in DMEM or as a building block of CS, that becomes more evident after BG dissolution [66,67]. The bands at 1273 cm−1 and 830 cm−1, which appeared and intensified notably after one day of degradation test, can be associated with the carbonate functional group. In previous work, the formation of calcium carbonate and amorphous calcium phosphate on BG in DMEM (as a biological fluid for in-vitro testing) has been reported [69,72].

The FTIR results of HACS coatings signify the presence of intense phosphate peaks associated with HA at 1025 cm−1 (ν3) and a double peak at 562 cm−1 and 601 cm−1 (ν4) [42,73]. However, the intensity of these peaks declines smoothly yet continuously indicating a more sustainable dissolution behavior of HA compared to BG. Similar to the BGCS coating, the characteristic peaks of CS polymer in the initial HACS coating can be identified within the range of approximately 1320–1730 cm−1. In the HACS coating, the bands associated with newly formed precipitations (at ∼1643 cm−1, 1724 cm−1, 1275 cm−1, and 837 cm−1) became visible only after 10 days of immersion. Overall, the growth of precipitations on BGCS coatings proceeds faster, while the HACS coatings provide accessibility to the inorganic bioactive component (HA) for a longer period of time.

The time-dependent cumulative release of Al, Ti, and V ions from the metallic substrate measured by the ICP-MS technique is presented in Fig. 9 . The quantity of ions released from the Ti6Al4V substrate follows the order: Al ˃ Ti ˃ V, which is determined by the type and stability of metal oxides formed from different phases of the alloy. This release pattern has been reported in several papers [74–76]. The concentration of aluminum is not only higher in the oxide film than in the bulk alloy, but it is also more enriched at the oxide/liquid interface. Less stable passivity of Al in chloride-containing media such as DMEM, explains the high concentration of Al ions released compared to Ti and V ions [76]. On the other hand, vanadium does not take part in the formation of the surface oxide layer and thus is expected to be released in lower quantities [74].

The concentrations of ions released from substrates coated with BGCS were greater than for the HACS-coated and the uncoated samples. As discussed above, the degradation of chitosan was higher and faster in BGCS than in HACS coatings. As a result, the acidic byproducts lower the pH near the metallic substrate, which in turn can increase the corrosion rate and thus the concentration of ions released from Ti6Al4V alloy substrates [77]. On the other hand, the concentrations of metallic ions released from the plain and HACS-coated substrate follow a similarly close pattern. This behavior is in good agreement with the results of corrosion tests (reported in Section 3.3). In contrast, HACS coatings provided suitable protection from releasing metallic ions (especially for Ti6Al4V), which is comparable to the uniform and consolidated protective oxide layer that naturally grows on the polished Ti6Al4V surface in contact with a corrosive environment.

The fluid flow is a complex in-vivo parameter that has been shown to affect the degradation of biomaterials by inducing shear stress [78]. The biodegradation of electrophoretically deposited HACS and BGCS coatings on Ti6Al4V substrates in dynamic conditions was conducted using a fluid dynamic experimental setup (Fig. 2). The mass variation and optical microscopy inspections of coated specimens following one and four days of exposure to circulating DMEM are illustrated in Fig. 10 . Like the static degradation results, the BGCS coating degraded extensively after the first time point of the test (i.e., 24 h). Accordingly, mounting the BGCS samples in the dynamic setup to continue the test for a further three days did not cause a notable change in weight loss. On the other hand, HACS samples showed gaining traces of mass after one day of contact with circulating DMEM. The slight mass increment can be due to the polymer's hydration and swelling as initial steps of degradation and/or precipitations from the solution. It has been reported that adsorption from the fluid is faster and higher in dynamic conditions compared to static conditions [78]. Comparing the mass loss of the BGCS coating after one day of immersion in dynamic condition (mean: 2.14 ± 0.59 mg·cm−2) with that under static condition (mean: 1.53 ± 0.38 mg·cm−2) shows that the flowing fluid has accelerated the degradation of BGCS coatings.

Optical microscope images of coatings at each time point are shown in Fig. 10 (B, C). In the case of BGCS coatings, it seems that the remaining layer is mostly composed of CS depleted from BG microparticles. In contrast, the HACS coating preserved its structure even after 4 days of contact with the circulating DMEM. Beyond the differences in the crystalline nature of the particles, this result appears to be influenced by a strong attachment between HA particles and the CS matrix as well. High-magnification micrographs of the coatings–particularly for BGCS–revealed signs of erosion, which is likely induced by the shear stress exerted by the moving fluid. The average wall shear stress (WSS) on samples by the fluid flow in a chamber with a rectangular cross-section can be calculated through the following equation [78,79]:
(2)
τ w
=
6 μQ
H 2
W
where μ is the dynamic viscosity of the fluid (for DMEM without any supplement is ca. 0.731 mPa·s [80]), Q is the flow rate (mL·min−1), and H and W refers to the height and width (cm) of the chamber.

According to the expression above, the average shear stress that was applied to samples in this study was calculated to be 5.71 mPa. Mechanical stimuli are considered a vital factor that regulates the remodeling and growth of bone tissue. The mechanical signals originate from different sources; one of which is the shear stress from body fluids [81]. The estimation of the WSS in bone tissue is a complicated procedure, as its biomechanical environment has been less defined, and the geometry of bone channels continuously changes over the remodeling cycle. Nevertheless, researchers have estimated the magnitude of shear stress from fluids on bone tissues from only a few mPa to more than one Pascal [81,82]. Interestingly, it has been found that better-organized bone apatite crystals can form under low WSS [83].

This result also indicates that the fluid dynamic set-up used in this study has simulated physiological conditions with respect to mechanical stimulation. This effect provides further information for investigators using a similar fluid-dynamic setup with a focus on studying the effect of mechanical force by moving fluids on the degradation of developed coatings.

Fig. 11 presents SEM images from BGCS- and HACS-coated substrates after one and four days of exposure to the circulating flow of DMEM. In line with previous outcomes, SEM images confirmed the fast dissolution of BG particles (Fig. 11 (A)). However, the presence of CS together with precipitations in the form of white granules can be detected after three days. This morphology is comparable to a similar coating system (58S BG/Chitosan) [22] that was incubated in a static DMEM solution for 7 days. Confirming the observations via optical microscope, the HACS coating has remained nearly intact even after 4 days of exposure to the flowing DMEM in the fluid-dynamic system (Fig. 11 (B)). A close-up analysis of HACS coating's microstructure elucidates robust cohesion between the globular HA particles and the CS matrix (Fig. 11 (C)). As explained earlier (Section 3.1), the surface morphology of HA particles exhibits very fine pores (Fig. 3 (D)), which can be formed as a result of clustering HA nanocrystals into spherical microparticles over the production process. The CS polymer appears to infiltrate these tiny pores and firmly bind the particles together and to the substrate. However, ruptures in some binding sites could be spotted in the coatings after the dynamic biodegradation test (Fig. 11 (D)).

The concentration of metallic ions released from Ti6Al4V substrates covered by either HACS or BGCS composite coatings during the dynamic biodegradation test is presented in Table 3 . As could be expected, the concentrations of metallic ions released from BGCS-coated substrates were higher compared to that from HACS-coated ones. Complying with previous studies [74–76], aluminum possessed the highest share of the released ions from Ti6Al4V alloys. The quantity of Al released from BGCS samples increased notably after 3 days, whereas it slightly declined for HACS coated samples possibly due to further precipitations on the coating as confirmed by gravimetric analysis. The Al in the protective oxide layer, which naturally grows on Ti6Al4V, is more concentrated at the interface with the liquid phase [76]. Furthermore, based on corrosion studies (Section 3.3), it is postulated that this oxide layer in BGCS samples is highly unstable. Additionally, the fluid flow condition can accelerate the oxidation and deactivation processes, thereby leading to the abundant release of Al-containing species. Although Al is reported to have a role in the physiological action of a few enzymes, at concentrations higher than 100 μg/L, a toxic effect of this element can appear [84]. The acceptable concentration of Ti in human serum is reported to be 3.3 μg/L [75], while the amount of titanium ions released from BGCS and HACS samples is well below this limit. These values are certainly dependent on a combination of factors such as the surface area of the implant, coating mass, and the total volume of the body fluid. Considering the average blood volume in an adult, i.e., ∼5 L of blood, it is expected that concentration values in vivo will be even lower [28].

The open circuit potential (OCP) curves of HACS and BGCS coatings, as well as the uncoated Ti6Al4V substrate are depicted in Fig. 12 . The values of equilibrium potential for HACS coatings (13 mV) and Ti6Al4V without coating (18 mV) are close and nobler than that of the BGCS coating at −179 mV. The stabilization of OCP for all samples occurred in a very short time, indicating fast growth of the passive layer establishing an equilibrium state between samples and the electrolyte [85]. The more positive OCP values show a more effective (thicker, denser, and defect-less) oxide layer has been formed on the surface of the sample [17,86]. The close values of OCP in HACS coated with the uncoated sample indicate a high degree of uniformity in HACS coating and its proper adhesion to the substrate compared to BGCS coating, in which the local flaws could advance percolation of the corrosive medium to the under-layer metal, thus inducing accelerated local corrosion reactions [87]. Although the results of electrochemical tests are influenced by many factors like the electrolyte, types and surface area of electrodes, temperature, etc., a rough comparison with the results from analogous coating systems previously reported [10,88,89], shows that the potentials recorded for the uncoated and HACS-coated samples in our study are notably nobler. This confirms that the fine polishing of the substrate had a noticeably affirmative influence on improving the corrosion resistance of Ti6Al4V alloy, as it was previously approved by Burnat et al. [90]. Accordingly, it is evident that the performance of composite coatings in improving corrosion resistance has been affected by the substrate surface pretreatment.

The curves obtained by the PDP test of the uncoated Ti6Al4V substrate and those covered by HACS and BGCS coatings in DMEM solution are shown in Fig. 13 . The corrosion parameters obtained by applying the Tafel extrapolation technique on the relevant PDP curves are reported in Table 4 . A corroding system can be classified as passive or non-passive depending on the behavior under anodic polarization. The reason for setting a wider range for anodic polarization (up to 2 V) was to study the passivation behavior of the samples and the conditions under which pitting corrosion may occur. As anodic polarization continues, the kinetics of corrosion slows down and becomes independent of the polarization (known as passivation region) due to the formation of a stable TiO2 film [91]. The course of the backward curve proves that anodic polarization caused an increase in the oxide layer thickness [90]. Although the passivation potential of BGCS coatings is comparable to that of uncoated and HACS-coated samples, the range of its active zone in anodic polarization is notably larger, which can stem from barriers against growing a uniform protective oxide layer on the metal surface. Furthermore, the indentations in the passive zone of the BGCS coating curve represent an unstable passivation behavior. As the anodic polarization proceeds, the curves slope towards increasing corrosion current. This behavior is a sign of the beginning and progress of pitting corrosion [92]. The corrosion potentials (Ecorr) of all samples are in the negative zone, signifying the active electrochemical behavior of Ti6Al4V and the formation of a protective oxide layer. However, comparing the polarization data (Table 4) shows the corrosion potential has shifted to a more active value, and the kinetics of the electrochemical reactions (icorr) have increased in coated samples, correlate well with the release studies of the specific ions (Ti, Al, and V). Corrosion rates (CR) of samples in millimeter per year can be calculated according to the ASTM-G102-23 procedure and using the equation below [90,93]:
(3)
CR = K
i corr
ρ
EW
where: K = 3.27 × 10−3 mm·g/μA·cm·yr, i
corr = corrosion current density (μA/cm2), ρ = density (g/cm3), and EW = equivalent weight.

Equivalent weight is a dimensionless parameter and is defined as the atomic weight of an element per valency. In the case of alloys, EW is the sum of those alloying elements with above 1 wt% by mass (Eq. (4)):
(4)

f i

where: n
i = the valency of the ith element, f

One strong hypothesis that explains the high corrosion resistance of the uncoated substrate is the surface pretreatment performed on the substrate towards a mirror-polished surface. According to the literature, the polishing process highly improves the corrosion resistance of metals [90,94]. Burnat et al. [90] showed polishing the surface of titanium alloys (Ti6Al4V and Ti6Al7Nb) reduces the corrosion rate more than three times compared to sandblasted samples. Passive current density (ipass) and breakdown potential (Ebd) are considered when analyzing the corrosion of metals with passivation behavior. Like the corrosion current density (icorr), the highest ipass was measured for BGCS, and the uncoated substrate possessed a passive film with higher protection behavior. With further increase of potential in the passive region, the anodic current density begins to rise rapidly. This point is equivalent to the initiation of the breakdown of the passive film, resulting in localized corrosion, such as pitting [95].

Analyzing the microstructure of surfaces after the polarization test provides insight into superficial defects in the failure of corrosion protection [86]. Fig. 14 (A) shows the microstructure of the initial Ti6Al4V surface consisting of the lamellar α and β phases [96]. The pores formed as a result of pitting corrosion could be detected on the surface oxide layer after the PDP process (Fig. 14 (B)). Fig. 14 (C and D) displays the EDS elemental mappings of the Ti6Al4V substrate covered with BGCS and HACS composite coatings after the PDP tests. The distribution of Ti, as a representative element of the metallic substrate, Ca and P for HACS, and Si as the indicative elements for BGCS coatings are presented to compare the substrate's coverage after the corrosion test. The exposed area of Ti6Al4V to the corrosive medium in BGCS coatings is larger than in HACS samples. Additionally, the colored dots areas belonging to titanium element (violet) have overlapped with the regions covered by the silicon elements (yellow dots). This observation indicates that the dispersion of mineral phase particles in BGCS coatings is not as uniformly compact as in HACS coatings.

Under anodic polarization, the system enters a passive region in which a multi-layered structure consisting of the composite coating, and the oxide layer on Ti governs the passive current density [97,98]. Degradation initiates either through microscopic coating defects [99] or localized swelling/delamination of chitosan, providing preferential channels for chloride ingress. Once chloride ions reach the metal–coating interface, they promote localized breakdown of the TiO2 layer [98,99]. The extent to which the breakdown is resisted depends on the cohesion and adhesion of the composite layer and the ability of HA/BG to buffer acidic micro-environments and re-healing defects, promoting the re-sealing of the passive layer [97,98].

Overall, the PDP results showed that the composite coatings did not provide additional protection against corrosion of a mirror-polished Ti6Al4V in DMEM solution; a very conducive environment for corrosion reactions. Nevertheless, improvements in the corrosion resistance of titanium alloys coated with CS, BG, and HA systems in other physiological-like solutions have been reported [32,87,100,101]. On the other hand, there are studies showing that applying composite coatings based on chitosan can shift the polarization curve towards a more active region [87,102,103]. After all, it is hypothesized that the penetration of DMEM through the microstructural gaps could lead to localized corrosion of Ti6Al4V and thus accelerate the electrochemical reaction rate [89]. When the oxide layer forms on the exposed areas, they act as a cathode, and as long as a reliable protective layer has not formed on the entire surface, they accelerate the electrochemical reactions [104]. Similarly, Dalili et al. [56] observed that incorporation of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) to HA coatings reduces the level of corrosion protection offered by the HA coating alone. This outcome was attributed to the formation of micro-pores around CNTs after the coatings were dried.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a non-destructive test that provides information on corrosion protection mechanisms in electrochemical systems by employing electrical elements such as resistors and capacitors to model the results of the relevant EIS plots [10]. The respective Nyquist and bode plots (bode-phase and bode-modulus) of the coated and uncoated Ti6Al4V after EIS test in DMEM solution are presented in Fig. 15 . The Nyquist plots reveal imperfect semicircles, which usually form due to charge transfer, effects of the coating layer, and mass transfer [69,105]. The bode plots (Fig. 15 (B, C)) have typical characteristics of the bode representations for titanium alloys [90,91]. In the bode-phase diagram, phase values of all samples show an increase up to ∼80° in the medium to low frequencies, confirming excellent corrosion resistance and slower reactivity [56,86]. A plateau in this region represents the formation of a passive oxide film that grows on the Ti6Al4V surface, which reflects semi-capacitive behavior. The two peaks detected for coated samples in this area reveal the bilayer nature of the surface [56,102]. Similarly, in the bode-modulus graph, increasing the impedance values towards lower frequencies shows an improvement in corrosion resistance due to the formation of a passive layer on the surface. As can be seen, this protection behavior is slightly higher in uncoated compared to coated samples, which is probably due to the formation of a uniform, dense, and defect-free oxide layer on the surface of polished Ti6Al4V [56,90]. Altogether, the Nyquist and bode plots indicated the formation and growth of a passive film with high protection behavior. There is an inverse relationship between the passive film thickness (L) and its capacitance according to the formula below [105]:
(5)
L =
ε
ε 0
A
C
where ε and ε 0 are the dielectric constant of the passive film and vacuum permittivity, respectively; A is the effective surface area; and C denotes the capacitance of the passive film.

Electrochemical phenomena can be studied and analyzed by an equivalent electrical circuit (EEC). For this purpose, the impedance spectrum obtained from the EIS test is modeled with a simple circuit presenting values that are closely approximate with experimental data. Here, the EEC based on a single time constant (Randles circuit) was used to simulate the electrochemical corrosion phenomena at the interface of the uncoated sample with DMEM solution, while for the coated samples, additional resistance and capacitance elements (a time-constant) were included to equate the effect of coating layers. The corresponding EEC and fitted values of electrical elements are presented in Fig. 16 and Table 5 , respectively. These circuits are aligned with previous studies [32,56,106,107], in which experimental EIS results of similar coating systems were modeled. In the EEC, Rs is the solution resistance and is influenced by the ionic strength of the electrolyte solution, surface area of the working electrode, and its distance from the reference electrode [56, 108]. As these parameters were kept constant over the test, the Rs did not change significantly (Table 5). Rb and CPEb are associated with an inner barrier oxide layer in the metal-coating interface, formed by the interaction of the attacking ions from the electrolyte with the metal substrate. In coated samples, Rc and CPEc correspond to the resistance and the constant phase element of the coating layer [56,90,106]. The electrochemical behavior of porous layers with non-smooth surface does not correspond to the ideal capacitance element and thus it is represented by a constant phase element (CPE). The impedance of CPE is calculated through the equation below [108,109]:
(6)
Z CPE
=
Y 0
− 1
.
jω
− n
in which Y 0 is the admittance constant (F·Sn−1); ω is the angular frequency (rad·s−1); j is an imaginary unit (j2 = −1); and n is the empirical exponent of CPE that ranges between 0 and 1, which quantifies deviation from an ideal capacitor (n = 1). The capacitance is usually approximated by the admittance (Y0) value obtained from the fitting and simulation process when the n value exceeds 0.8 [86,91].

According to corrosion resistance values (Table 5), it can be concluded that the corrosion protection of samples is dominated by their barrier oxide layer (Rb), and that the uncoated substrate possesses the highest value. Considering the corrosion resistance of coatings (Rc), it is evident that HACS had a better performance than BGCS coatings. This outcome is in good agreement with PDP results, indicating that the uniform oxide layer formed on the polished uncoated Ti6Al4V substrate acted as a more effective barrier against corrosion in DMEM solution [56,90,108].

A systematic investigation of biodegradation (under static and dynamic conditions) and corrosion protection of advanced composite BGCS and HACS coatings developed by EPD in DMEM physiological solution was carried out. The starting materials of the coatings, prepared suspensions for the EPD process, and the final coatings were comprehensively characterized and compared. HACS suspension was found to be more suitable for EPD under consistent experimental conditions. The HACS suspension had higher colloidal stability than the BGCS suspension. In contrast, the current density during EPD in the BGCS suspension was remarkably higher (about 8 times) than its counterpart inducing the formation of localized irregularities in the microstructure, despite taking advantage of H2O2 in the suspension preparation.

The rate of biodegradation in BGCS coatings was significantly higher than in HACS coatings in both static and dynamic conditions. This outcome was explained by the faster degradation of chitosan in BGCS coatings compared to HACS coatings, microstructural gaps in BGCS versus the robust structure of HACS coatings, and the faster dissolution of BG compared to the sustained dissolution and release of HA particles. Dynamic fluid accelerated degradation of BGCS coatings and resulted in discernible erosion due to shear stress, whereas the structure of HACS coatings was preserved as the HA particles were firmly bound together and to the substrate by CS thanks to the surface morphology of HA spheroids.

Although HACS coatings had far better anticorrosion performance than BGCS coatings, both types of coatings were unable to offer additional protection for uncoated Ti6Al4V against corrosion in an aggressive solution like DMEM. It was found that the surface pretreatment (mirror-polished) chosen for the substrates endows superb corrosion resistance to the uncoated Ti6Al4V, while local flaws and surface irregularities in the coatings led to unstable passivation behavior and accelerated local electrochemical reactions.

Overall, to provide multifunctionalities of advanced organic-inorganic coatings for Ti-implants, choosing an appropriate surface pretreatment and achieving flawless microstructures are key requirements. Indisputably, there is great potential for further research on EPD-derived composite coatings such as those developed in this study, exploiting the relative advantages and potential disadvantages of BG and HA as bioactive filler in CS.
